critique_points.point_001: The paper lacks sufficient comparison with related work on curriculum and data warm-up strategies that manipulate data complexity or difficulty, which would clarify the novelty of isolating surface-level text complexity.
critique_points.point_002: The semantic preservation of simplified texts is only measured via embedding cosine similarity without human evaluation or stronger semantic alignment metrics, raising concerns about LLM hallucinations or subtle content shifts.
critique_points.point_003: Pretraining was conducted for only one epoch, which may be insufficient for models to fully learn from the data and for robust conclusions about complexity effects.
critique_points.point_004: The downstream evaluation is limited to classification and multiple-choice tasks, omitting generative or open-ended tasks, thus limiting generalizability to real-world language model usage.
critique_points.point_005: The study omits baselines pretrained on mixed or randomly shuffled complexity paragraphs, which would help quantify the benefit of exposure to complexity diversity.
critique_points.point_006: Zero-shot results are confounded by small dataset size and model scale, hindering clear interpretation of complexity effects on zero-shot transfer.
critique_points.point_007: The choice of MobileLLM architecture is not justified, and it is unclear if the findings generalize to more common transformer or encoder-decoder models.
critique_points.point_008: Reproducibility is hindered by missing details on the LLM simplification prompting protocol, pretraining hyperparameters, optimizer settings, and compute budgets.

summary: This paper investigates the impact of surface-level text complexity on language model pretraining by creating parallel corpora of human-written and LLM-simplified text with preserved semantics. Models pretrained on simplified text perform comparably to those on human-written text for fine-tuning tasks but show slight deficits on zero-shot benchmarks involving world knowledge and entity tracking. Smaller models benefit more from simplified data in perplexity, while larger models are more robust to complexity differences. The findings suggest text complexity plays a nuanced role, being less critical for fine-tuning but important for zero-shot and knowledge-intensive tasks, thereby offering practical insights for dataset curation and synthetic data generation.
strengths: Creation of a unique parallel dataset isolating surface-level text complexity while preserving semantic content.
Comprehensive evaluation across model sizes and a range of downstream fine-tuning and zero-shot tasks.
weaknesses: Limited pretraining duration (one epoch) and small dataset size may restrict generalizability to large-scale training.
Narrow evaluation scope focusing on classification and multiple-choice tasks, excluding generative and encoder-based model architectures.
questions: How might the findings change if pretraining were conducted for multiple epochs or on substantially larger corpora?
Would the observed effects of text complexity hold for encoder or encoder-decoder models and for generative downstream tasks?
title: Improving Language Model Pretraining with Text Structure Information
